text:
Bacterial wound infections constitute a major global health challenge due to increasing levels of antibiotic-resistant bacteria. Here, we report an antimicrobial strategy towards rapid wound healing without the need for antibiotics, based on a bimetallic hybrid nanozyme. A novel iron oxide cores ultra-small gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) embedded into an IRMOF-3 shell as the Fe3O4@MOF@Au NPs (FMA NPs) composite were constructed for the first time.

The FMA nanozymes had a synergistically enhanced peroxidase (POD) activity, and could catalyze low concentrations of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 0.97 mM) into highly reactive hydroxyl radical (·OH). In vitro, hybrid nanozymes showed excellent biocompatibility while demonstrated strong bactericidal activities towards both Gram-negative (Escherichia coli) and Gram-positive bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus) by destroying biofilms. In a mouse wound infected model, the FMA NPs promoted rapid wound healing with high biosafety and no systematic toxicity. Overall, our findings demonstrated that FMA hybrid nanozymes had great potential in treating antibiotic-resistant bacterial infection and improving wound healing therapy in a clinical application without using any antibiotics.
